one_line_takeaway: GOAT shows that a general-purpose, unaligned attacker LLM that reasons and dynamically layers known jailbreak techniques over just 5 conversational turns can jailbreak frontier models at up to ~97% ASR@10 in a pure closed-box setting, outperforming the Crescendo multi-turn baseline everywhere.
key_finding: GOAT achieves high attack success within a tiny (5-turn) query budget and consistently beats the Crescendo multi-turn baseline across all 5 target models. Headline: ASR@10 of 96.5% (~97%) against Llama 3.1 8B and 87.9% (~88%) against GPT-4-Turbo on JailbreakBench. Multi-turn dramatically outperforms single-turn (ASR@1 is far lower than ASR@10 for every model), and success rises with conversation turns. GOAT is also cheaper per turn than judge-in-the-loop methods (~3x fewer LLM calls, since the attacker self-reasons instead of calling a separate judge each turn) and needs no gradients or thousand-query search. In successful runs against Llama 3.1, GOAT overwhelmingly opens with Hypothetical and Persona Modification, then diversifies to Topic Splitting, Response Priming, and Dual Response in later turns.
core_contribution: The paper introduces GOAT (Generative Offensive Agent Tester), a fully automated, closed-box agentic red-teaming system that jailbreaks target LLMs through multi-turn natural-language conversations rather than a single optimized adversarial prompt. GOAT's own novelty is threefold: (1) it automates human-style multi-turn red teaming by having a general-purpose 'helpful-only' attacker LLM dynamically pick and layer from a toolbox of 7 prompt-level jailbreak techniques mid-conversation; (2) it uses a Chain-of-Attack-Thought reasoning scheme (a Chain-of-Thought variant) where the attacker emits four fields each turn - Observation, Thought, Strategy, and the actual Reply sent to the target; (3) the attack toolbox is extensible purely via in-context learning (attack name + definition + exemplars in the system prompt), so no adversarial fine-tuning of the attacker is needed to add a new technique. This is distinct from the cited prior work it explicitly positions against - gradient/suffix attacks (Zou et al. GCG), single-prompt closed-box search (Chao et al. PAIR, Mehrotra et al. TAP), and the single most comparable baseline, Crescendo (Russinovich et al.), a multi-turn gradual-escalation attack that GOAT reimplements and outperforms.
threat_model: Adversary is an ordinary-to-moderately-sophisticated user of a deployed chat LLM with only closed-box, query-level access through the normal user interface: no model weights, no gradients, no logits, no fine-tuning of the target. The attacker interacts over multiple conversational turns (capped at 5 here) to elicit content that violates the target's safety/policy training (the paper's harm goals include e.g. a poem glorifying the Holocaust/Nazi party). The attacker component is itself an LLM with limited/no safety alignment; the paper frames GOAT as a defensive tool for model developers to scale internal red teaming, but the mechanism is a genuine jailbreak-generation capability.
limitations: The authors flag: (1) Context-window limit - capping at 5 turns to fit Llama 2 7B's 4096 context handicaps some attacks; they note ASR could be higher against longer-context models. (2) Performance is tightly coupled to the attacker model's quality - it must lack safety alignment enough to comply and have strong reasoning/instruction-following for long complex system prompts; weaker attackers sometimes respond to the attack strategy text rather than executing it. Reviewer-added: the judge is a single automated Llama-3.0-70B classifier (no reported human-agreement numbers in this paper), and 'any unsafe message counts' is a permissive success criterion; the attacker model is an unnamed proprietary Meta 'helpful-only' model, limiting reproducibility; the exact attack definitions and exemplars are deliberately withheld from Table 1 for misuse reasons, so the toolbox is not fully reproducible; only 5 target models and 100 behaviors are evaluated; no defense/mitigation is actually implemented and tested.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (Chao et al. 2024) - 100 behaviors, used as the harm-goal set and its associated judge
Referenced as prior/source datasets that JailbreakBench distills: AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023, 500 instructions) and HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024)
quantitative_results: Crescendo ASR@10 (baseline): GPT-3.5 = 80.4%, GPT-4 = 70.9%, Llama 2 7B = 72.0%, Llama 3 8B = 75.6%, Llama 3.1 8B = 84.5%
Crescendo ASR@1 (baseline): GPT-3.5 = 40.0%, GPT-4 = 31.7%, Llama 2 7B = 22.6%, Llama 3 8B = 24.2%, Llama 3.1 8B = 37.7%
Query budget: max 5 conversation turns per run; GOAT uses ~3x fewer LLM calls per turn than judge-in-the-loop methods (no separate per-turn judge)
baselines_compared: Crescendo (Russinovich et al. 2024) - multi-turn gradual-escalation jailbreak, reimplemented with the same helpful-only attacker and same 5-request budget; GOAT beats it on ASR@1 and ASR@10 for all 5 targets
Positioned against (not head-to-head reproduced) single-prompt methods: GCG/gradient-suffix (Zou et al.), PAIR (Chao et al.), TAP (Mehrotra et al.)
